html:
CeoTronics AG, LS:540740

CeoTronics is a German micro-cap defense and public-safety electronics supplier that has just moved from obscure headset maker to backlog-backed Bundeswehr modernization beneficiary. At about EUR 11.70 on Lang & Schwarz, the equity value is roughly EUR 93 million. This is not the stale “single-digit P/E today” story from older writeups. The cleaner thesis is better: current-year valuation is fair-to-cheap, but the market is not fully pricing the EUR 47 million Rheinmetall/Bundeswehr SmG call-off that will be recognized mainly in FY2026/27 and FY2027/28.

The company’s own H1 FY2025/26 report showed revenue up 61.6% to EUR 34.2 million, EBIT up 107.1% to EUR 5.8 million, and net income up 113.4% to EUR 3.9 million. Management guides FY2025/26 revenue to about EUR 56 million and consolidated net income to at least EUR 5.3 million, implying EPS near EUR 0.66 and a current-year P/E around 17.5x. That is not screaming cheap in isolation.

The variant perception is that FY2025/26 is the wrong earnings year. In January, CeoTronics announced the largest order in its history: more than EUR 47 million from Rheinmetall Electronics for Bundeswehr soldier-system communication equipment, with deliveries spread across the next two fiscal years. BankM’s January update lifted its fair value to EUR 19.10, while Montega’s December update raised its target to EUR 17.00. More importantly, those notes frame a 2026/27 and 2027/28 earnings bridge, not a one-year spike.

The real business is not generic audio hardware. CeoTronics sells rugged communication interfaces, push-to-talk controls, wireless control buttons, headset systems, bodycam/video products, and tactical communication accessories for police, fire services, military, industrial, and aviation users. The key role is the human-machine interface in noisy, dangerous environments where failure is unacceptable. That creates procurement friction, qualification cycles, training lock-in, and repeat parts demand. A headset or PTT button is a small share of a soldier-system budget, but a failure can break the system in the field. That is a good niche.

The market’s first mistake is treating CeoTronics as a lumpy Scale-segment micro-cap. Some lumpiness is real. Government orders arrive in blocks, working capital can swing, and disclosure is sparse. But the backlog has become too large to ignore: after the Rheinmetall call-off, CeoTronics reported orders on hand of EUR 75.8 million as of January 13, 2026. For a company guiding EUR 56 million of annual revenue, that is unusually strong visibility.

The second mistake is underestimating the framework structure. The SmG framework can cover up to 191,000 units through 2030. The January call-off represented another 50,000 PTT buttons, 50,000 wireless PTT buttons, 50,000 storage bags, and 12,500 connection cables. With 110,000 units called off to date, there is still optionality in the remaining framework volume, before considering broader European defense and public-safety modernization.

The third mistake is missing the pullback. Retail forums and German small-cap boards have noticed the stock, but the debate is still thin, volume-sensitive, and focused on near-term chart moves. The share price has backed off from early-2026 enthusiasm despite the fundamental setup improving. That is exactly where this kind of ignored defense-tech supplier becomes interesting: not pre-discovery, but post-first-spike, when investors who bought the headline exit before the revenue recognition shows up in reported EPS.

The forward valuation is the core. On company guidance, FY2025/26 net income of EUR 5.3 million is about 17.5x earnings. But if the business earns EUR 7.5 to EUR 8.5 million in FY2026/27 or FY2027/28 as the large call-off converts, the stock is only 11x to 12.5x forward earnings. That is too low for a debt-light, backlog-covered defense electronics niche with a structural procurement tailwind. It is especially too low when the nearest public-market comparison, Invisio, usually receives a much richer defense-communications multiple.

This is not an asset-light software story, and it should not be sold as one. CeoTronics still manufactures physical products, invests in capacity, and will consume working capital during ramps. But the better label is “specialized defense electronics compounder with option value,” not “headset maker.” The company is expanding production and administration space, has a long operating history, and pays a dividend. Management’s decision to reinvest in capacity while raising the dividend to EUR 0.20 is rational capital allocation, not cash hoarding.

The next catalysts are straightforward. FY2025/26 results should confirm the step-up in profitability. More important, the company needs to show that the large Rheinmetall order starts flowing into FY2026/27 revenue without margin disappointment. Any further call-off under the SmG framework, additional police/video orders, or international defense wins would force investors to model a longer earnings plateau. A move to a more visible listing segment would also matter, but the thesis does not need it.

The bear case is procurement concentration. Rheinmetall/Bundeswehr exposure is now large, and a delay can shift revenue between fiscal years. Margin could also disappoint if the company underprices scale work, adds headcount too early, or runs into supply-chain bottlenecks. The stock is illiquid and listed in a market segment many institutions ignore. Those are real risks. But the balance sheet is not fragile, the order book is real, and the product sits in a mission-critical niche where displacement is harder than the company’s size suggests.

My base case is that CeoTronics earns roughly EUR 0.90 to EUR 1.00 per share within two fiscal years, then keeps enough backlog and framework visibility to deserve 18x earnings. That supports a stock around EUR 19. The bull case is that SmG follow-on volume and European public-safety modernization push revenue toward EUR 80 million plus, net margin settles in the mid-teens, EPS approaches EUR 1.50, and the market values CeoTronics as a scarce defense-communications asset. That supports EUR 32 without needing a takeout.

At today’s approximate EUR 11.70 price:

Bear target: EUR 7.50, down 36%
Orders roll off, FY2027 net income falls back toward EUR 4.5 to EUR 5.0 million, and the market applies a 12x micro-cap industrial multiple.

Base target: EUR 19.00, up 62%
The Rheinmetall backlog converts, EPS reaches about EUR 1.00, and the stock earns an 18x to 19x defense-electronics multiple.

Bull target: EUR 32.00, up 174%
The SmG framework continues, additional European defense/public-safety orders arrive, EPS approaches EUR 1.50, and CeoTronics re-rates toward specialized defense-tech peers.
